# Asset pricing econometrics

```{r}
# This removes all items in environment.
rm(list = ls())

library(tidyquant)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyr)
library(purrr)
library(readr)
library(lubridate)
library(broom)
library(ggplot2)
library(tibble)
library(knitr)
library(kableExtra)
library(scales)
```

## Asset pricing as an econometric problem

Asset pricing models connect expected excess returns to systematic sources of
risk. The econometric task is to estimate that connection from data. In this
chapter, the test assets are mutual-fund returns and the 25 Fama-French
size-value portfolios. The risk factors are the market excess return, size,
value, momentum, profitability, and investment factors from the Kenneth French
Data Library.

The chapter adapts the original Fama-French material in the `FF` source folder
into the book's current format. The source folder is left untouched. The chapter
downloads the data during execution, fixes the sample end at 2020-12-31 to
preserve the original empirical window, and rebuilds the workflow with a clearer
bridge between the model equations, the code, and the interpretation.

The central object is an excess return:

$$
R_{i,t}^e
=
R_{i,t}-R_{f,t},
$$

where $R_{i,t}$ is the return on asset $i$ in month $t$ and $R_{f,t}$ is the
risk-free return. A factor model explains $R_{i,t}^e$ using common sources of
variation. The CAPM uses one factor, the market excess return
[@sharpe1964capital; @lintner1965valuation; @mossin1966equilibrium]. The
Fama-French three-factor model adds size and value factors
[@fama1992cross; @fama1993common]. Momentum and the five-factor model extend
the factor set [@carhart1997persistence; @fama2015five].

The workflow is:

$$
\text{factor returns}
\longrightarrow
\text{test assets}
\longrightarrow
\text{time-series regressions}
\longrightarrow
\text{alphas and betas}
\longrightarrow
\text{cross-sectional fit}.
$$

That sequence matters. A factor model combines regression evidence with an
economic claim about which risks should explain average excess returns.

## Data, downloads, and sample construction

The factor data and the 25 size-value portfolio returns are downloaded from the
Kenneth French Data Library [@kennethFrenchDataLibrary]. The individual test
asset example uses the Fidelity Contrafund ticker `FCNTX` downloaded with
`tidyquant`. All returns are converted from percentages to decimal units during
estimation. Percent units are used only when a table or graph is meant for
interpretation.

```{r asset-pricing-data}
sample_end <- as.Date("2020-12-31")

ff_urls <- list(
  ff3 = "https://mba.tuck.dartmouth.edu/pages/faculty/ken.french/ftp/F-F_Research_Data_Factors_CSV.zip",
  momentum = "https://mba.tuck.dartmouth.edu/pages/faculty/ken.french/ftp/F-F_Momentum_Factor_CSV.zip",
  ff5 = "https://mba.tuck.dartmouth.edu/pages/faculty/ken.french/ftp/F-F_Research_Data_5_Factors_2x3_CSV.zip",
  portfolios_25 = "https://mba.tuck.dartmouth.edu/pages/faculty/ken.french/ftp/25_Portfolios_5x5_CSV.zip"
)

download_ff_csv <- function(url, skip) {
  zip_file <- tempfile(fileext = ".zip")
  unzip_dir <- tempfile()
  dir.create(unzip_dir)

  download_ok <- tryCatch(
    {
      utils::download.file(url, zip_file, mode = "wb", quiet = TRUE, method = "libcurl")
      TRUE
    },
    error = function(error) FALSE,
    warning = function(warning) FALSE
  )

  if (!download_ok && nzchar(Sys.which("curl.exe"))) {
    status <- system2(
      Sys.which("curl.exe"),
      c("-L", "-s", "-o", zip_file, url),
      stdout = TRUE,
      stderr = TRUE
    )
    download_ok <- file.exists(zip_file) && file.info(zip_file)$size > 0
  }

  if (!download_ok) {
    stop("Could not download Fama-French data from: ", url, call. = FALSE)
  }

  utils::unzip(zip_file, exdir = unzip_dir)

  csv_file <- list.files(unzip_dir, pattern = "\\.csv$|\\.CSV$", full.names = TRUE)

  if (!length(csv_file)) {
    stop("No CSV file found in downloaded archive: ", url, call. = FALSE)
  }

  readr::read_csv(csv_file[[1]], skip = skip, show_col_types = FALSE, progress = FALSE)
}

parse_ff_monthly <- function(raw_data) {
  names(raw_data)[1] <- "date_raw"

  date_text <- trimws(as.character(raw_data$date_raw))
  first_blank <- which(is.na(raw_data$date_raw) | date_text == "")[1]

  if (!is.na(first_blank) && first_blank > 1) {
    raw_data <- raw_data[seq_len(first_blank - 1), ]
  }

  raw_data |>
    mutate(
      date_raw = trimws(as.character(date_raw)),
      date = lubridate::ceiling_date(lubridate::ymd(paste0(date_raw, "01")), "month") -
        lubridate::days(1)
    ) |>
    select(date, everything(), -date_raw) |>
    mutate(across(-date, ~ as.numeric(.x) / 100)) |>
    rename_with(~ gsub("-", "_", .x, fixed = TRUE))
}

first_last_rows <- function(data, n = 3) {
  idx <- unique(c(seq_len(min(n, nrow(data))), seq(max(nrow(data) - n + 1, 1), nrow(data))))
  data[idx, , drop = FALSE]
}

ff3 <- download_ff_csv(ff_urls$ff3, skip = 3) |>
  parse_ff_monthly() |>
  filter(date <= sample_end)

momentum <- download_ff_csv(ff_urls$momentum, skip = 13) |>
  parse_ff_monthly() |>
  filter(date <= sample_end)

ff5 <- download_ff_csv(ff_urls$ff5, skip = 3) |>
  parse_ff_monthly() |>
  filter(date <= sample_end, !is.na(RMW), !is.na(CMA))

portfolios_25 <- download_ff_csv(ff_urls$portfolios_25, skip = 15) |>
  parse_ff_monthly() |>
  filter(date <= sample_end)

ff3_momentum <- ff3 |>
  inner_join(momentum, by = "date") |>
  rename(SMB3 = SMB) |>
  relocate(RF, .after = Mom)

ff5_extra <- ff5 |>
  select(date, SMB, RMW, CMA) |>
  rename(SMB5 = SMB)

all_factors <- ff3_momentum |>
  inner_join(ff5_extra, by = "date") |>
  relocate(RF, .after = CMA)

factor_windows <- bind_rows(
  ff3 |> summarise(source = "Fama-French 3 factors", start = min(date), end = max(date), months = n()),
  momentum |> summarise(source = "Momentum factor", start = min(date), end = max(date), months = n()),
  ff5 |> summarise(source = "Fama-French 5 factors", start = min(date), end = max(date), months = n()),
  portfolios_25 |> summarise(source = "25 size-value portfolios", start = min(date), end = max(date), months = n())
)

```

The code keeps the data in decimal form. For example, a monthly return of
`0.01` means one percent. This convention prevents unit mistakes when returns
are added, multiplied, or used in regressions.

The original material shows the first and last observations of the main data
objects. Those checks are included here because they verify dates, decimal
units, and column names before any model is estimated.

```{r data-sample-tables}
first_last_rows(ff3) |>
  kable(
    caption = "First and last observations of the Fama-French three-factor data.",
    digits = 6,
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

first_last_rows(momentum) |>
  kable(
    caption = "First and last observations of the momentum factor.",
    digits = 6,
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

first_last_rows(ff5) |>
  kable(
    caption = "First and last observations of the Fama-French five-factor data.",
    digits = 6,
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

first_last_rows(all_factors) |>
  kable(
    caption = "First and last observations of the combined factor data.",
    digits = 6,
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

first_last_rows(portfolios_25) |>
  select(date, `SMALL LoBM`, `SMALL HiBM`, `BIG LoBM`, `BIG HiBM`) |>
  kable(
    caption = "First and last observations of selected 25-portfolio corner returns.",
    digits = 6,
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

These tables are a data audit. The first and last rows show that the factor
files and the portfolio file are monthly series, expressed in decimal units,
and aligned on month-end dates. The selected corner portfolios already hint at
the empirical structure used later: small growth, small value, big growth, and
big value are the extreme cells of the size-value grid.

## Excess returns and factor notation

The CAPM time-series regression for asset $i$ is

$$
R_{i,t}^e
=
\alpha_i
+
\beta_{i,M} MKT_t
+
\varepsilon_{i,t},
$$

where $MKT_t$ is the market excess return. The intercept $\alpha_i$ is the
average excess return that remains after controlling for market exposure. In a
well-specified asset pricing model, alphas should be economically small.

The Fama-French three-factor model is

$$
R_{i,t}^e
=
\alpha_i
+
\beta_{i,M} MKT_t
+
\beta_{i,S} SMB_t
+
\beta_{i,H} HML_t
+
\varepsilon_{i,t}.
$$

The factors have direct economic interpretations:

| Factor | Interpretation |
|---|---|
| $MKT_t$ | Market excess return. |
| $SMB_t$ | Size factor: small minus big. |
| $HML_t$ | Value factor: high book-to-market minus low book-to-market. |
| $MOM_t$ | Momentum factor: recent winners minus recent losers. |
| $RMW_t$ | Profitability factor: robust minus weak profitability. |
| $CMA_t$ | Investment factor: conservative minus aggressive investment. |

The five-factor model replaces the three-factor specification with

$$
R_{i,t}^e
=
\alpha_i
+
\beta_{i,M} MKT_t
+
\beta_{i,S} SMB_t
+
\beta_{i,H} HML_t
+
\beta_{i,R} RMW_t
+
\beta_{i,C} CMA_t
+
\varepsilon_{i,t}.
$$

The code names these variables as `Mkt_RF`, `SMB3`, `HML`, `Mom`, `SMB5`,
`RMW`, and `CMA`. The distinction between `SMB3` and `SMB5` is intentional:
the size factor in the three-factor data and the size factor in the five-factor
data are constructed from different portfolio sorts.

## Factor portfolios through time

Before estimating asset pricing regressions, it is useful to inspect the factor
returns themselves. The next helper computes annualized return, annualized
volatility, and a simple return-to-risk ratio.

```{r factor-performance}
annualized_performance <- function(data, cols) {
  data |>
    select(date, all_of(cols)) |>
    pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "factor", values_to = "return") |>
    filter(!is.na(return)) |>
    group_by(factor) |>
    summarise(
      months = n(),
      annualized_return = prod(1 + return)^(12 / months) - 1,
      annualized_volatility = sd(return) * sqrt(12),
      return_per_unit_risk = annualized_return / annualized_volatility,
      .groups = "drop"
    ) |>
    arrange(return_per_unit_risk)
}

factor_perf <- annualized_performance(ff3_momentum, c("Mkt_RF", "SMB3", "HML", "Mom"))

factor_perf |>
  mutate(across(c(annualized_return, annualized_volatility, return_per_unit_risk), ~ round(.x, 4))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Annualized performance of the three Fama-French factors and momentum.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The annualized table turns each factor into an object that can be compared like
a portfolio. The return column shows the long-run reward, the volatility column
shows the risk of the factor strategy, and the return-per-risk column gives a
compact scale for ranking factor performance before looking at individual
assets.

The three-factor file is inspected first by itself. This mirrors the original
workflow: read the Fama-French factors, check their time path, and then add
momentum as an extension.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff3-cumulative
#| fig-cap: "Cumulative returns of the Fama-French three factors."
ff3 |>
  select(date, Mkt_RF, SMB, HML, RF) |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "factor", values_to = "return") |>
  group_by(factor) |>
  mutate(cumulative_return = cumprod(1 + return)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(date, cumulative_return, color = factor)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::number_format(accuracy = 0.1)) +
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Cumulative return, log scale", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The cumulative-return plot emphasizes that factor premia are earned through
time, with long expansions and reversals. A factor can have a positive average
return and still experience long drawdowns, so the time path is part of the
economic story.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff3-distribution
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of monthly returns for the Fama-French three factors."
ff3 |>
  select(date, Mkt_RF, SMB, HML) |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "factor", values_to = "return") |>
  ggplot(aes(return, fill = factor)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45, adjust = 2.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "gray30") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-0.2, 0.2)) +
  labs(x = "Monthly return", y = "Density", fill = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The density plot complements the cumulative plot. It shows where monthly
returns are concentrated and how far negative or positive observations can
extend. This helps students read factor performance as both a distribution of
monthly outcomes and an annualized average.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff-factor-risk-return
#| fig-cap: "Risk-return profile of the factor portfolios."
factor_perf |>
  ggplot(aes(annualized_volatility, annualized_return, color = return_per_unit_risk)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray70", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, color = "#B22222", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = factor), vjust = -0.9, color = "black", size = 3.5) +
  labs(
    x = "Annualized volatility",
    y = "Annualized return",
    color = "Return per unit of risk"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "C") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The risk-return profile compresses the table into one visual comparison. Points
above the red reference line have annualized returns larger than annualized
volatility on this scale. Labels make clear which factor drives each position,
so the graph can be read as a map of factor rewards and risks.

Cumulative factor returns show that factors are portfolios through time. The
line for each factor is the wealth path from investing one dollar in that
long-short factor portfolio:

$$
W_{j,t}
=
\prod_{s=1}^{t}
(1+F_{j,s}).
$$

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff-factor-cumulative
#| fig-cap: "Cumulative returns of Fama-French factors and momentum."
ff3_momentum |>
  select(date, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML, Mom) |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "factor", values_to = "return") |>
  group_by(factor) |>
  mutate(cumulative_return = cumprod(1 + return)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(date, cumulative_return, color = factor)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::number_format(accuracy = 0.1)) +
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Cumulative return, log scale", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

Adding momentum changes the visual comparison because the set of candidate
factor portfolios is now broader. The purpose of the figure is to show how the
market, size, value, and momentum factor histories differ before any asset
pricing regression uses them as explanatory variables.

The distribution of monthly factor returns is another diagnostic. Momentum and
value can have long episodes of strong performance and sharp reversals, so a
factor's average return should always be read together with its volatility and
tail behavior.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff-factor-distribution
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of monthly factor returns."
ff3_momentum |>
  select(date, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML, Mom) |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "factor", values_to = "return") |>
  ggplot(aes(return, fill = factor)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45, adjust = 2.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "gray30") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-0.2, 0.2)) +
  labs(x = "Monthly return", y = "Density", fill = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The monthly distributions also show why factor regressions can be sensitive to
large episodes. A factor with a similar average return can have a different
tail profile, and that affects how individual assets load on it in a regression.

The same workflow can be applied to the five-factor data. The shorter sample
starts in 1963, because the profitability and investment factors are available
from that point in the downloaded file.

```{r ff5-performance}
ff5_perf <- annualized_performance(ff5, c("Mkt_RF", "SMB", "HML", "RMW", "CMA"))

ff5_perf |>
  mutate(across(c(annualized_return, annualized_volatility, return_per_unit_risk), ~ round(.x, 4))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Annualized performance of the Fama-French five factors.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The five-factor table prepares the reader for a different factor set and a
shorter historical window. Profitability and investment are interpreted as
additional dimensions of systematic risk, so their return and volatility should
be inspected before they are added to any regression.

The original workflow also inspects the five-factor sample and the combined
factor set with momentum. This matters because the samples differ: the
five-factor data start later, and the `SMB` factor in the five-factor file is a
different constructed series from the `SMB` factor in the three-factor file. The
chapter keeps the variable names `SMB3` and `SMB5` to make that distinction
visible in the code.

```{r additional-factor-performance}
all_factor_perf <- annualized_performance(
  all_factors,
  c("Mkt_RF", "SMB3", "HML", "Mom", "SMB5", "RMW", "CMA")
)

all_factor_perf |>
  mutate(across(c(annualized_return, annualized_volatility, return_per_unit_risk), ~ round(.x, 4))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Annualized performance of the combined factor set.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The combined table is mainly a bookkeeping and comparison device. It gathers
all factor candidates used later in the chapter and makes the sample change
visible. A model with more factors is easier to estimate mechanically, but the
reader still needs to know which factor histories are being compared.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff5-risk-return
#| fig-cap: "Risk-return profile of the Fama-French five factors."
ff5_perf |>
  ggplot(aes(annualized_volatility, annualized_return, color = return_per_unit_risk)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray70", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, color = "#B22222", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = factor), vjust = -0.9, color = "black", size = 3.3) +
  labs(
    x = "Annualized volatility",
    y = "Annualized return",
    color = "Return per unit of risk"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "C") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The five-factor risk-return graph shows whether the added profitability and
investment factors occupy distinct positions relative to market, size, and
value. If two factors have similar risk-return profiles, the regression may
still distinguish them through covariance with asset returns.

```{r}
#| label: fig-all-factor-risk-return
#| fig-cap: "Risk-return profile of all factors used in the chapter."
all_factor_perf |>
  ggplot(aes(annualized_volatility, annualized_return, color = return_per_unit_risk)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray70", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, color = "#B22222", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = factor), vjust = -0.9, color = "black", size = 3.1) +
  labs(
    x = "Annualized volatility",
    y = "Annualized return",
    color = "Return per unit of risk"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "C") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The all-factor graph is the broadest factor map in the chapter. It summarizes
the candidate explanatory variables before the chapter turns to test assets,
where these factor histories become regressors.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff5-cumulative
#| fig-cap: "Cumulative returns of the Fama-French five factors."
ff5 |>
  select(date, Mkt_RF, SMB, HML, RMW, CMA) |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "factor", values_to = "return") |>
  group_by(factor) |>
  mutate(cumulative_return = cumprod(1 + return)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(date, cumulative_return, color = factor)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::number_format(accuracy = 0.1)) +
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Cumulative return, log scale", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The cumulative five-factor plot lets the reader compare the newer factors in
their available sample. The shorter window should be kept in mind when comparing
these paths with the earlier three-factor histories.

```{r}
#| label: fig-all-factor-cumulative
#| fig-cap: "Cumulative returns of all factors used in the chapter."
all_factors |>
  select(date, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML, Mom, SMB5, RMW, CMA) |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "factor", values_to = "return") |>
  group_by(factor) |>
  mutate(cumulative_return = cumprod(1 + return)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(date, cumulative_return, color = factor)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::number_format(accuracy = 0.1)) +
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Cumulative return, log scale", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The cumulative all-factor figure is useful for seeing which factors dominate
visually and which have more modest paths. This is a descriptive step; the
regression step later asks whether these paths help explain asset returns.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff5-distribution
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of monthly returns for the Fama-French five factors."
ff5 |>
  select(date, Mkt_RF, SMB, HML, RMW, CMA) |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "factor", values_to = "return") |>
  ggplot(aes(return, fill = factor)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45, adjust = 2.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "gray30") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-0.2, 0.2)) +
  labs(x = "Monthly return", y = "Density", fill = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The five-factor density plot shows how each factor behaves month by month.
Students should read it together with the cumulative plot: the same factor can
look attractive cumulatively while still having large monthly downside episodes.

```{r}
#| label: fig-all-factor-distribution
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of monthly returns for all factors used in the chapter."
all_factors |>
  select(date, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML, Mom, SMB5, RMW, CMA) |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "factor", values_to = "return") |>
  ggplot(aes(return, fill = factor)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45, adjust = 2.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "gray30") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-0.2, 0.2)) +
  labs(x = "Monthly return", y = "Density", fill = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The final factor-density plot closes the descriptive block. At this point, the
reader has seen factor returns as tables, cumulative paths, risk-return points,
and monthly distributions. The next step is to use those factor returns to
explain the behavior of test assets.

## Test assets and the size-value portfolios

The chapter uses two kinds of test assets. The first is `FCNTX`, a single mutual
fund. The second is the 25 Fama-French size-value portfolios. The single asset
is useful for seeing one regression closely. The 25 portfolios are useful for
testing whether the model explains a structured cross-section of returns.

```{r asset-and-portfolio-data}
ticker <- "FCNTX"

fund_prices <- tq_get(
  ticker,
  from = "1980-01-01",
  to = sample_end,
  get = "stock.prices"
)

fund_returns <- fund_prices |>
  group_by(symbol) |>
  tq_transmute(
    select = adjusted,
    mutate_fun = periodReturn,
    period = "monthly",
    col_rename = "returns"
  ) |>
  ungroup()

fund_factors <- fund_returns |>
  filter(date <= max(all_factors$date)) |>
  mutate(date = lubridate::ceiling_date(date, "month") - lubridate::days(1)) |>
  left_join(all_factors, by = "date") |>
  mutate(Re = returns - RF) |>
  select(symbol, date, returns, Re, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML, Mom, SMB5, RMW, CMA, RF)

portfolio_long <- portfolios_25 |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "symbol", values_to = "returns")

portfolio_labels <- function(symbols) {
  tibble(symbol = symbols) |>
    mutate(
      Size = case_when(
        grepl("^SMALL|^ME1", symbol) ~ "Small",
        grepl("^ME2", symbol) ~ "2",
        grepl("^ME3", symbol) ~ "3",
        grepl("^ME4", symbol) ~ "4",
        grepl("^BIG|^ME5", symbol) ~ "Big",
        TRUE ~ NA_character_
      ),
      Value = case_when(
        grepl("LoBM|BM1", symbol) ~ "Low",
        grepl("BM2", symbol) ~ "2",
        grepl("BM3", symbol) ~ "3",
        grepl("BM4", symbol) ~ "4",
        grepl("HiBM|BM5", symbol) ~ "High",
        TRUE ~ NA_character_
      ),
      Size = factor(Size, levels = c("Small", "2", "3", "4", "Big")),
      Value = factor(Value, levels = c("Low", "2", "3", "4", "High"))
    )
}

portfolio_label_tbl <- portfolio_labels(unique(portfolio_long$symbol))
label_portfolios <- c("SMALL LoBM", "SMALL HiBM", "BIG LoBM", "BIG HiBM")

portfolio_factor_data <- portfolio_long |>
  filter(date <= max(ff3_momentum$date)) |>
  inner_join(ff3_momentum, by = "date") |>
  mutate(Re = returns - RF) |>
  select(date, symbol, returns, Re, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML, Mom) |>
  left_join(portfolio_label_tbl, by = "symbol")

portfolio_all_factor_data <- portfolio_long |>
  filter(date <= max(all_factors$date)) |>
  inner_join(all_factors, by = "date") |>
  mutate(Re = returns - RF) |>
  select(date, symbol, returns, Re, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML, Mom, SMB5, RMW, CMA) |>
  left_join(portfolio_label_tbl, by = "symbol")

fund_factors |>
  select(symbol, date, returns, Re, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML, Mom) |>
  slice(c(1:3, (n() - 2):n())) |>
  kable(
    caption = "FCNTX monthly returns joined to factor returns.",
    digits = 6,
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

This table is the first bridge between an individual asset and the factor data.
The column `returns` is the raw FCNTX return, while `Re` subtracts the risk-free
rate. The regression will use `Re` as the dependent variable and factor returns
as explanatory variables, so this joined table is the dataset for the first
asset-pricing exercise.

The source material checks the single asset before estimating the regression.
The price table verifies the data source. The return table verifies the monthly
transformation. The joined table verifies that the excess return and factors are
aligned at month end.

```{r fcntx-data-checks}
first_last_rows(fund_prices) |>
  select(symbol, date, adjusted, close, volume) |>
  kable(
    caption = "First and last FCNTX price observations.",
    digits = 6,
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

first_last_rows(fund_returns) |>
  kable(
    caption = "First and last FCNTX monthly raw returns.",
    digits = 6,
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

first_last_rows(fund_factors) |>
  select(symbol, date, returns, Re, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML, Mom, SMB5, RMW, CMA, RF) |>
  kable(
    caption = "First and last FCNTX excess returns joined to all factors.",
    digits = 6,
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

first_last_rows(portfolio_factor_data) |>
  select(date, symbol, returns, Re, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML, Mom) |>
  kable(
    caption = "First and last 25-portfolio excess-return rows joined to FF3 and momentum.",
    digits = 6,
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

first_last_rows(portfolio_all_factor_data) |>
  select(date, symbol, returns, Re, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML, Mom, SMB5, RMW, CMA) |>
  kable(
    caption = "First and last 25-portfolio excess-return rows joined to the expanded factor set.",
    digits = 6,
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The sequence of data checks has a simple logic. First, prices become monthly
returns. Second, raw returns become excess returns. Third, each excess return is
matched to factor returns for the same month. The 25-portfolio checks repeat
the same process for many test assets instead of a single mutual fund.

```{r fcntx-factor-performance}
fund_factor_returns <- fund_factors |>
  select(date, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML, Mom, SMB5, RMW, CMA, Re) |>
  rename(FCNTX = Re)

fund_factor_perf <- annualized_performance(
  fund_factor_returns,
  c("Mkt_RF", "SMB3", "HML", "Mom", "SMB5", "RMW", "CMA", "FCNTX")
)

fund_factor_perf |>
  mutate(across(c(annualized_return, annualized_volatility, return_per_unit_risk), ~ round(.x, 4))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Annualized performance of FCNTX excess returns and the factors.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The FCNTX performance table places the mutual fund and factor portfolios on the
same scale. This helps answer a descriptive question before estimation: does
the fund look more volatile, less volatile, or more rewarded per unit of risk
than the factors used to explain it?

```{r}
#| label: fig-fcntx-factor-risk-return
#| fig-cap: "Risk-return profile of FCNTX excess returns and the factors."
fund_factor_perf |>
  ggplot(aes(annualized_volatility, annualized_return, color = return_per_unit_risk)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray70", linewidth = 0.4) +
  geom_abline(intercept = 0, slope = 1, color = "#B22222", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text(aes(label = factor), vjust = -0.9, color = "black", size = 3.1) +
  labs(
    x = "Annualized volatility",
    y = "Annualized return",
    color = "Return per unit of risk"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "C") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The risk-return figure makes the same comparison visually. FCNTX appears as one
point among the factor portfolios, which helps the reader see whether the fund
is close to market-like behavior or whether it sits in a different region of
the return-risk space.

```{r}
#| label: fig-fcntx-factors-cumulative
#| fig-cap: "Cumulative excess returns of FCNTX and the factor portfolios."
fund_factor_returns |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "series", values_to = "return") |>
  group_by(series) |>
  mutate(cumulative_return = cumprod(1 + return)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(date, cumulative_return, color = series)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::number_format(accuracy = 0.1)) +
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Cumulative return, log scale", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The cumulative plot turns the FCNTX comparison into a time-series story. A fund
can have a similar average return to a factor while following a different path
through time. That difference is exactly what the regression tries to summarize
with an intercept, beta, and residual.

```{r}
#| label: fig-fcntx-market-cumulative
#| fig-cap: "Cumulative excess returns of FCNTX and the market factor."
fund_factor_returns |>
  select(date, Mkt_RF, FCNTX) |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "series", values_to = "return") |>
  group_by(series) |>
  mutate(cumulative_return = cumprod(1 + return)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(date, cumulative_return, color = series)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::number_format(accuracy = 0.1)) +
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Cumulative return, log scale", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The market comparison isolates the CAPM idea. If FCNTX is mostly a market-risk
asset, the FCNTX and market excess-return paths should move together in broad
strokes. Differences between the paths foreshadow the residual component in the
CAPM regression.

```{r}
#| label: fig-fcntx-market-distribution
#| fig-cap: "Distribution of monthly excess returns for FCNTX and the market factor."
fund_factor_returns |>
  select(date, Mkt_RF, FCNTX) |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "series", values_to = "return") |>
  ggplot(aes(return, fill = series)) +
  geom_density(alpha = 0.45, adjust = 2.5) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "gray30") +
  coord_cartesian(xlim = c(-0.2, 0.2)) +
  labs(x = "Monthly excess return", y = "Density", fill = NULL) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The distribution plot adds another view of the same comparison. Overlap in the
two densities suggests similar monthly-return behavior, while differences in
spread or tails suggest that market exposure alone may leave unexplained
variation.

The 25 portfolios are organized by size and book-to-market. Their average raw
returns form a surface. This is the visual reason the portfolios are useful for
asset pricing: a model should explain systematic differences across size and
value cells.

The same 25 portfolios can be read as investment portfolios through the usual
risk-return lens. The original material uses annualized return, annualized
volatility, and return per unit of risk to show the economic content of the
size-value grid. Each cell is a portfolio with its own average return and
volatility.

```{r portfolio-performance}
portfolio_perf <- portfolio_long |>
  group_by(symbol) |>
  summarise(
    months = n(),
    annualized_return = prod(1 + returns)^(12 / months) - 1,
    annualized_volatility = sd(returns) * sqrt(12),
    return_per_unit_risk = annualized_return / annualized_volatility,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(return_per_unit_risk) |>
  left_join(portfolio_label_tbl, by = "symbol")

portfolio_perf |>
  select(symbol, annualized_return, annualized_volatility, return_per_unit_risk) |>
  slice(c(1:5, (n() - 4):n())) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Lowest and highest return-risk ratios among the 25 size-value portfolios.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The table shows the extremes of the 25-portfolio risk-return ranking. This is a
compact way to see that the size-value grid contains meaningful heterogeneity:
some portfolios deliver much more return per unit of volatility than others.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff25-risk-return
#| fig-cap: "Risk-return profile of the 25 size-value portfolios."
portfolio_perf |>
  ggplot(aes(annualized_volatility, annualized_return, color = return_per_unit_risk)) +
  geom_point(aes(size = return_per_unit_risk), alpha = 0.85, show.legend = FALSE) +
  geom_text(aes(label = symbol), vjust = -0.9, size = 2.4, color = "black") +
  labs(
    x = "Annualized volatility",
    y = "Annualized return",
    color = "Return per unit of risk"
  ) +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_color_viridis_c(option = "C") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The risk-return scatter makes the heterogeneity visible across all 25
portfolios. Dense labels are intentional here because each point is a named
test asset. The graph prepares the reader for the asset-pricing question: can
factor exposures explain why these portfolios occupy different locations?

Kenneth French's data library often illustrates the extreme size-value
portfolios because they make the empirical pattern easy to see. The plot below
keeps the four corner portfolios from the original material: small growth,
small value, big growth, and big value.

```{r}
#| label: fig-kf-hilo-cumulative
#| fig-cap: "Cumulative raw returns for the four corner size-value portfolios."
portfolios_25 |>
  select(date, `SMALL HiBM`, `BIG HiBM`, `SMALL LoBM`, `BIG LoBM`) |>
  filter(date >= as.Date("1927-07-31"), date <= as.Date("1999-12-31")) |>
  pivot_longer(-date, names_to = "portfolio", values_to = "return") |>
  mutate(
    portfolio = factor(
      portfolio,
      levels = c("SMALL HiBM", "BIG HiBM", "BIG LoBM", "SMALL LoBM")
    )
  ) |>
  group_by(portfolio) |>
  mutate(cumulative_return = cumprod(1 + return)) |>
  ungroup() |>
  ggplot(aes(date, cumulative_return, color = portfolio)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 0.8) +
  scale_y_log10(labels = scales::number_format(accuracy = 0.1)) +
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Cumulative return, log scale", color = NULL) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "right")
```

The four-corner cumulative plot focuses on the most interpretable cells of the
grid. Comparing small versus big and low versus high book-to-market portfolios
shows why the size-value sort became a standard test bed for factor models.

The source material also included rendered 3D assets of the mean-return
surface. The chapter keeps the surface render and adds the original animation
as a web video: the height and color of each cell represent the average monthly
raw return of that portfolio.

```{r portfolio-surface-table}
portfolio_means <- portfolio_long |>
  left_join(portfolio_label_tbl, by = "symbol") |>
  group_by(Size, Value) |>
  summarise(mean_return = mean(returns, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

portfolio_surface_data <- portfolio_means |>
  mutate(
    ME = as.integer(Size),
    BM = as.integer(Value),
    raw_return_percent = 100 * mean_return
  ) |>
  select(ME, BM, raw_return_percent)

portfolio_surface_data |>
  arrange(ME, BM) |>
  mutate(raw_return_percent = round(raw_return_percent, 4)) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Data used to build the size-value mean-return surface.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The surface table gives the numeric values behind the rendered image. It is the
same information in tabular form: `ME` moves from small to big firms, `BM` moves
from low to high book-to-market firms, and `raw_return_percent` is the average
monthly raw return in that cell.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff25-surface-original
#| fig-cap: "Rendered surface of the 25 size-value portfolio mean returns."
#| out-width: 60%
#| fig-align: center
knitr::include_graphics("figures/ff-p25-surface.png")
```

The rendered surface is a visual summary of the size-value pattern. Peaks mark
cells with higher average monthly returns; lower blocks mark cells with lower
average returns. The point of the figure is to make the cross-sectional pattern
visible before the regression tries to explain it with factor exposures.

```{=html}
<figure class="quarto-float">
  <video controls autoplay loop muted playsinline preload="metadata" style="width: min(100%, 520px); display: block; margin: 0 auto;">
    <source src="figures/ff-p25-surface.mp4" type="video/mp4">
  </video>
  <figcaption>Animated rendered surface of the 25 size-value portfolio mean returns.</figcaption>
</figure>
```

The animation rotates the same surface. It uses the same data and helps the
reader see the three-dimensional structure of the 25 portfolio averages.

## Time-series asset pricing regressions

The time-series regression estimates factor exposures for each asset. In matrix
form, for asset $i$ and $K$ factors,

$$
R_{i,t}^e
=
\alpha_i
+
\boldsymbol{\beta}_i^\top
\mathbf{f}_t
+
\varepsilon_{i,t}.
$$

The code below wraps that equation in a reusable function. The formula defines
the right-hand side. The grouping variable `symbol` defines which asset receives
its own regression.

```{r regression-functions}
fit_factor_model <- function(data, formula) {
  data |>
    group_by(symbol) |>
    nest() |>
    mutate(
      model = map(data, ~ lm(formula, data = .x)),
      tidy = map(model, broom::tidy),
      glance = map(model, broom::glance),
      augmented = map2(model, data, ~ broom::augment(.x, data = .y))
    ) |>
    ungroup()
}

tidy_factor_model <- function(model_tbl) {
  model_tbl |>
    select(symbol, tidy) |>
    unnest(tidy)
}

glance_factor_model <- function(model_tbl) {
  model_tbl |>
    select(symbol, glance) |>
    unnest(glance)
}

augment_factor_model <- function(model_tbl) {
  model_tbl |>
    select(symbol, augmented) |>
    unnest(augmented)
}
```

For `FCNTX`, the CAPM regression is

$$
R_{\text{FCNTX},t}^e
=
\alpha_{\text{FCNTX}}
+
\beta_{\text{FCNTX},M} MKT_t
+
\varepsilon_{\text{FCNTX},t}.
$$

```{r fcntx-capm}
fund_capm <- fit_factor_model(fund_factors, Re ~ Mkt_RF)

tidy_factor_model(fund_capm) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 6))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "CAPM time-series regression for FCNTX.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The CAPM table has two central entries. The market coefficient is the estimated
market beta of FCNTX: it measures how much the fund's excess return changes
with the market excess return. The intercept is the fund's average excess
return after controlling for market exposure.

The fitted value is the model-implied excess return for each month:

$$
\widehat{R}_{i,t}^e
=
\widehat{\alpha}_i
+
\widehat{\beta}_{i,M}MKT_t.
$$

```{r}
#| label: fig-fcntx-capm-fit
#| fig-cap: "FCNTX excess returns and CAPM fitted values."
augment_factor_model(fund_capm) |>
  ggplot(aes(date)) +
  geom_col(aes(y = Re, fill = "Observed excess return"), alpha = 0.5) +
  geom_col(aes(y = .fitted, fill = "CAPM fitted value"), alpha = 0.5) +
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Monthly excess return", fill = NULL) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The fitted-value plot shows the CAPM as a month-by-month approximation. Months
where the fitted bars and observed bars are close are months where market
exposure explains much of the fund's movement. Large gaps are residuals: the
part of the FCNTX excess return left after the market factor is used.

The original material then checks whether the fitted values and residuals look
reasonable. These graphs are diagnostics for the time-series fit. They ask
whether the CAPM regression is fitting the single asset in a stable way before
the chapter moves to the 25-portfolio cross-section.

```{r}
#| label: fig-fcntx-capm-observed-fitted
#| fig-cap: "Observed and fitted FCNTX excess returns."
fund_capm_aug <- augment_factor_model(fund_capm)

fund_capm_aug |>
  ggplot(aes(Re, .fitted)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.75) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "#D55E00", linewidth = 0.8) +
  coord_fixed() +
  labs(x = "Observed excess return", y = "Fitted excess return") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

The observed-versus-fitted scatter compresses the time-series fit into one
plane. Points near the 45-degree line indicate months where the CAPM fitted
return is close to the observed FCNTX excess return. The spread around the line
shows the size of month-level errors.

```{r}
#| label: fig-fcntx-capm-residuals-fitted
#| fig-cap: "FCNTX residuals against fitted values."
fund_capm_aug |>
  ggplot(aes(.fitted, .resid)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray60", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.75) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", formula = y ~ x, se = FALSE, linewidth = 0.8) +
  labs(x = "Fitted excess return", y = "Residual") +
  scale_x_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

The residuals-against-fitted graph checks whether errors change systematically
with the fitted return. A visible slope or curved pattern would suggest that the
single market factor is leaving structure in the residuals.

```{r}
#| label: fig-fcntx-capm-residuals-time
#| fig-cap: "FCNTX CAPM residuals through time."
fund_capm_aug |>
  ggplot(aes(date, .resid)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray60", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2C7FB8", alpha = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Date", y = "Residual") +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = scales::percent) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

The residual time plot shows whether unexplained returns cluster in particular
periods. Clusters matter because they suggest that the model misses episodes
where FCNTX behaves differently from the market factor.

```{r}
#| label: fig-fcntx-capm-residual-acf
#| fig-cap: "Autocorrelation function for FCNTX CAPM residuals."
fund_residual_acf <- stats::acf(fund_capm_aug$.resid, lag.max = 24, plot = FALSE)

tibble(
  lag = as.numeric(fund_residual_acf$lag),
  acf = as.numeric(fund_residual_acf$acf)
) |>
  filter(lag > 0) |>
  ggplot(aes(lag, acf)) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0, color = "gray60", linewidth = 0.5) +
  geom_col(fill = "#2C7FB8", alpha = 0.75) +
  labs(x = "Lag", y = "ACF") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

The autocorrelation plot asks whether residuals are serially related. In a clean
time-series regression, residuals should have little predictable structure
across lags. Large bars would indicate persistence in the unexplained component.

```{r fcntx-residual-tests}
fund_capm_aug |>
  group_by(symbol) |>
  summarise(
    ljung_box_p_value = Box.test(.resid, lag = 10, type = "Ljung-Box", fitdf = 2)$p.value,
    box_pierce_p_value = Box.test(.resid, lag = 10, type = "Box-Pierce", fitdf = 2)$p.value,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 6))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Residual autocorrelation checks for the FCNTX CAPM regression.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The Ljung-Box and Box-Pierce p-values formalize the residual-autocorrelation
check. They summarize whether residual autocorrelation is large enough to be a
statistical concern at the chosen lag.

For the 25 portfolios, the CAPM estimates 25 market betas:

```{r portfolio-capm}
portfolio_capm <- fit_factor_model(portfolio_factor_data, Re ~ Mkt_RF)

tidy_factor_model(portfolio_capm) |>
  filter(term == "Mkt_RF") |>
  arrange(estimate) |>
  slice(c(1:5, (n() - 4):n())) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 6))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Lowest and highest CAPM market betas among the 25 portfolios.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The beta table shows the range of market exposures across the 25 portfolios.
This range is important because cross-sectional asset pricing needs variation
in exposures. If all portfolios had nearly identical market betas, market beta
would have little power to explain differences in average returns.

```{r portfolio-capm-full-table}
tidy_factor_model(portfolio_capm) |>
  arrange(symbol, term) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 6))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "CAPM coefficients for all 25 size-value portfolios.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The full coefficient table keeps the individual portfolio estimates visible.
Each portfolio has its own intercept and market beta, so the CAPM is estimated
as 25 separate time-series regressions, one for each test asset.

The 25 separate CAPM regressions have different levels of fit. A compact way to
inspect them is to compare $R^2$, residual standard error, and residual
autocorrelation diagnostics across portfolios.

```{r portfolio-capm-diagnostics}
portfolio_capm_diagnostics <- glance_factor_model(portfolio_capm) |>
  select(symbol, r.squared, adj.r.squared, sigma, AIC, BIC) |>
  arrange(r.squared)

portfolio_capm_diagnostics |>
  slice(c(1:5, (n() - 4):n())) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Lowest and highest CAPM goodness-of-fit values among the 25 portfolios.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The goodness-of-fit table shows which portfolios are relatively well described
by the market factor and which are less well described. A higher $R^2$ means
that market movements explain a larger fraction of that portfolio's excess
return variation.

```{r portfolio-capm-residual-tests}
augment_factor_model(portfolio_capm) |>
  group_by(symbol) |>
  summarise(
    box_pierce_p_value = Box.test(.resid, lag = 10, type = "Box-Pierce", fitdf = 2)$p.value,
    .groups = "drop"
  ) |>
  arrange(box_pierce_p_value) |>
  slice(c(1:5, (n() - 4):n())) |>
  mutate(box_pierce_p_value = round(box_pierce_p_value, 6)) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Lowest and highest Box-Pierce p-values for the 25 CAPM residual series.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The residual test table extends the diagnostic logic from FCNTX to the full
portfolio set. Portfolios with very small p-values deserve attention because
their residuals show stronger serial structure under the CAPM specification.

## Asset pricing errors and factor exposures

The Fama-French three-factor model adds size and value exposures:

$$
R_{i,t}^e
=
\alpha_i
+
\beta_{i,M}MKT_t
+
\beta_{i,S}SMB_t
+
\beta_{i,H}HML_t
+
\varepsilon_{i,t}.
$$

The fitted expected excess return is obtained by taking the estimated
factor loadings and multiplying them by average factor returns. The intercept is
reported separately because, in asset pricing, alpha is the pricing error:

$$
\widehat{E}_{\text{factors}}(R_i^e)
=
\widehat{\beta}_{i,M}\bar{MKT}
+
\widehat{\beta}_{i,S}\bar{SMB}
+
\widehat{\beta}_{i,H}\bar{HML}.
$$

This equation is the bridge between the regression table and the asset-pricing
plot. The regression estimates betas. The asset-pricing restriction asks
whether those betas, without an asset-specific alpha, explain average excess
returns. If $\widehat{\alpha}_i$ is economically important, the factor model is
leaving a pricing error for asset $i$.

The distinction is important for interpretation. A time-series regression with
an intercept can always decompose the sample average return into two parts:

$$
\bar{R}_i^e
=
\widehat{\alpha}_i
+
\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_i^\top
\bar{\mathbf{f}}.
$$

The term
$\widehat{\boldsymbol{\beta}}_i^\top\bar{\mathbf{f}}$ is the return explained
by factor exposures. The term $\widehat{\alpha}_i$ is the part that remains
asset-specific after those exposures have been used. The asset-pricing question
is therefore direct: are the alphas small enough for the factor model to be a
credible explanation of average returns?

```{r ff3-expected-returns}
portfolio_ff3 <- fit_factor_model(portfolio_factor_data, Re ~ Mkt_RF + SMB3 + HML)
ff3_betas <- tidy_factor_model(portfolio_ff3)

expected_return_from_betas <- function(data, model_tbl, factors) {
  factor_means <- data |>
    summarise(across(all_of(factors), mean, na.rm = TRUE)) |>
    pivot_longer(everything(), names_to = "term", values_to = "factor_mean")

  average_returns <- data |>
    group_by(symbol) |>
    summarise(ERe = mean(Re, na.rm = TRUE), .groups = "drop")

  alpha_tbl <- tidy_factor_model(model_tbl) |>
    filter(term == "(Intercept)") |>
    transmute(symbol, alpha = estimate)

  factor_implied <- tidy_factor_model(model_tbl) |>
    filter(term != "(Intercept)") |>
    select(symbol, term, estimate) |>
    inner_join(factor_means, by = "term") |>
    group_by(symbol) |>
    summarise(factor_implied = sum(estimate * factor_mean), .groups = "drop")

  factor_implied |>
    inner_join(alpha_tbl, by = "symbol") |>
    inner_join(average_returns, by = "symbol") |>
    left_join(portfolio_label_tbl, by = "symbol") |>
    mutate(
      ERe_percent = 100 * ERe,
      factor_implied_percent = 100 * factor_implied,
      alpha_percent = 100 * alpha,
      pricing_error_percent = ERe_percent - factor_implied_percent
    )
}

ff3_expected <- expected_return_from_betas(
  portfolio_factor_data,
  portfolio_ff3,
  c("Mkt_RF", "SMB3", "HML")
)

```

This code block prepares the data for the figures that follow. It estimates the
three-factor model, stores each portfolio's factor exposures, computes the
factor-implied part of average return, and keeps alpha as the remaining
asset-specific component.

The size and value patterns can be inspected directly by reading average excess
returns against market beta by portfolio sort.

The market-beta plots deliberately ignore the fitted-return axis and return to
the raw relation between market exposure and average excess returns. If market
beta were enough to explain the 25 portfolios, higher beta portfolios would line
up with higher average returns in a stable pattern. The size and value groupings
show whether portfolios with similar market beta still differ because of their
position in the size-value grid.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff-market-beta-return
#| fig-cap: "Market beta and average excess returns for the 25 portfolios."
ff3_betas |>
  filter(term == "Mkt_RF") |>
  rename(market_beta = estimate) |>
  select(symbol, market_beta) |>
  inner_join(ff3_expected, by = "symbol") |>
  ggplot(aes(market_beta, ERe_percent)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.2) +
  labs(x = "Market beta", y = "Observed average excess return (%)") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

The plain market-beta graph is the starting point for the anomaly discussion.
If market beta alone organized average returns, the cloud would show a clear
upward relation. A scattered pattern motivates the size and value groupings in
the next two figures.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff-size-anomaly
#| fig-cap: "Market beta and average excess returns by size portfolio."
ff3_betas |>
  filter(term == "Mkt_RF") |>
  rename(market_beta = estimate) |>
  select(symbol, market_beta) |>
  inner_join(ff3_expected, by = "symbol") |>
  ggplot(aes(market_beta, ERe_percent, color = Size, group = Size)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.6, linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Size), color = "black", size = 3, show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Market beta", y = "Observed average excess return (%)", color = "Size") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The size plot connects each point to its size group. Lines that separate by
size indicate that portfolios with similar market beta can have different
average returns because they belong to different size cells.

```{r}
#| label: fig-ff-value-anomaly
#| fig-cap: "Market beta and average excess returns by value portfolio."
ff3_betas |>
  filter(term == "Mkt_RF") |>
  rename(market_beta = estimate) |>
  select(symbol, market_beta) |>
  inner_join(ff3_expected, by = "symbol") |>
  ggplot(aes(market_beta, ERe_percent, color = Value, group = Value)) +
  geom_line(alpha = 0.6, linewidth = 0.9) +
  geom_point(size = 2) +
  geom_text(aes(label = Value), color = "black", size = 3, show.legend = FALSE) +
  labs(x = "Market beta", y = "Observed average excess return (%)", color = "Value") +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")
```

The value plot reads the same beta-return relation through book-to-market
groups. Separation by value group is the visual motivation for adding the HML
factor to the model.

## Cross-sectional asset pricing tests

Time-series regressions estimate betas. Cross-sectional regressions ask whether
those betas explain average returns across assets. The beta-pricing equation is

$$
\bar{R}_i^e
=
\lambda_0
+
\lambda_M \widehat{\beta}_{i,M}
+
\lambda_S \widehat{\beta}_{i,S}
+
\lambda_H \widehat{\beta}_{i,H}
+
\eta_i.
$$

This is the second-pass logic associated with Fama-MacBeth style asset pricing
tests [@fama1973risk]. In this chapter, the cross-section is the set of 25
size-value portfolios.

The equation has a clear economic reading. The left-hand side,
$\bar{R}_i^e$, is the average excess return of portfolio $i$. The regressors are
estimated exposures from the first-pass time-series regressions. The
coefficients $\lambda_M$, $\lambda_S$, and $\lambda_H$ are prices of risk in the
cross-section. A positive $\lambda_H$ means that, holding market and size
exposure fixed, portfolios with higher value exposure have higher average
excess returns in this sample. A low fitted value for a portfolio means that
its estimated betas explain less than its observed average return under this
second-pass equation.

There are therefore two layers of estimation:

| Layer | Object estimated | Data orientation | Economic role |
|---|---|---|---|
| Time-series regression | $\alpha_i$ and $\boldsymbol{\beta}_i$ for each asset | One asset across many months | Measures exposure to factors |
| Cross-sectional regression | $\lambda$ coefficients | Many assets in one average-return cross-section | Prices those exposures |

The first layer asks how each portfolio moves with the factors. The second
layer asks whether those exposures explain why some portfolios earn higher
average excess returns than others.

```{r cross-sectional-beta-method}
beta_wide <- ff3_betas |>
  select(symbol, term, estimate) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = term, values_from = estimate) |>
  select(symbol, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML) |>
  rename(
    beta_market = Mkt_RF,
    beta_size = SMB3,
    beta_value = HML
  )

cross_section_data <- ff3_expected |>
  select(symbol, ERe_percent) |>
  inner_join(beta_wide, by = "symbol")

cross_section_fit <- lm(ERe_percent ~ beta_market + beta_size + beta_value, data = cross_section_data)

cross_section_data |>
  arrange(symbol) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 6))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Database for the beta-method cross-sectional regression.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

broom::tidy(cross_section_fit) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 6))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Cross-sectional regression using estimated betas.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

broom::glance(cross_section_fit) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 6))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Goodness-of-fit summary for the beta-method cross-sectional regression.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

```

The cross-sectional block should be read as a second pass. The first table is
the data matrix: one row per portfolio, average excess return as the outcome,
and estimated betas as regressors. The coefficient table estimates the prices
attached to those betas. The goodness-of-fit table summarizes how much of the
25-portfolio average-return pattern is captured by this beta-pricing equation.

```{r}
#| label: fig-cross-sectional-beta-fit
#| fig-cap: "Cross-sectional fitted returns using estimated betas."
cross_section_aug <- broom::augment(cross_section_fit, data = cross_section_data)

cross_section_aug |>
  left_join(portfolio_label_tbl, by = "symbol") |>
  ggplot(aes(.fitted, ERe_percent)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "#D55E00", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = symbol), size = 2.2) +
  coord_fixed(xlim = c(0.5, 1.65), ylim = c(0.5, 1.65)) +
  labs(
    x = "Cross-sectional fitted return (%)",
    y = "Observed average excess return (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

The fitted-return figure is the visual version of the second-pass regression.
Each label is one portfolio. Points close to the 45-degree line are portfolios
whose average returns are close to what the estimated beta prices predict.
Points above the line have observed average returns larger than the fitted
value; points below the line have observed average returns smaller than the
fitted value.

```{r cross-sectional-fitted-table}
cross_section_aug |>
  select(symbol, ERe_percent, fitted_return_percent = .fitted) |>
  arrange(symbol) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Observed and fitted average excess returns from the beta method.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The fitted-value table gives the same comparison portfolio by portfolio. It is
useful when labels overlap in the figure or when the reader wants to inspect a
particular size-value cell directly.

The coefficients $\lambda_M$, $\lambda_S$, and $\lambda_H$ are estimated prices
of risk. A positive $\lambda_H$, for example, means that portfolios with higher
value exposure have higher average excess returns in this cross-section,
conditional on the other beta estimates.

The fitted-return graph and fitted-value table should be read together. The
graph shows the overall geometry: points close to the 45-degree line are
well-explained by the beta-pricing equation. The table makes the individual
portfolio comparison auditable. Large vertical gaps indicate portfolios whose
average returns remain far from the fitted values implied by the estimated
prices of market, size, and value exposure.

## SDF moments and GMM logic

The stochastic discount factor view starts from the pricing condition

$$
E(m_t R_{i,t}^e)=0.
$$

This notation is standard in modern asset pricing and GMM treatments
[@cochrane2005asset; @hansen1982large].

The object $m_t$ is a stochastic discount factor. It assigns value today to
future payoffs. For an excess return, the cost of the payoff is zero after
financing at the risk-free rate, so the model-implied present value should also
be zero. That is the meaning of $E(m_t R_{i,t}^e)=0$: once returns are adjusted
by the discount factor, no test asset should have a remaining average payoff
that the model leaves unexplained.

A linear SDF writes

$$
m_t
=
a
-
\mathbf{b}^{\top}\mathbf{f}_t.
$$

This says that the discount factor moves with the same systematic factors used
earlier in the chapter. The vector $\mathbf{b}$ controls how strongly each
factor enters the SDF. If a factor captures bad times for investors, assets
that pay poorly when that factor is adverse should require higher average
excess returns. The SDF formulation and the beta-pricing formulation are two
ways to organize the same economic idea: systematic covariance with factors is
what matters for average returns.

With this specification, the empirical content comes from sample moments such
as

$$
\widehat{E}(R_i^e f_j)
=
\frac{1}{T}
\sum_{t=1}^{T}
R_{i,t}^e f_{j,t}.
$$

The moment $\widehat{E}(R_i^e f_j)$ is an average product. It is large when
portfolio $i$ tends to have high excess returns in months when factor $j$ is
high. It is negative when the portfolio tends to move against the factor. In
matrix form, the code builds these quantities with

$$
\mathbf{M}
=
\mathbf{R}^{\top}\mathbf{F},
$$

where $\mathbf{R}$ is a $T \times N$ matrix of portfolio excess returns and
$\mathbf{F}$ is a $T \times K$ matrix of factor returns. The result
$\mathbf{M}$ is an $N \times K$ matrix: one row for each test portfolio and one
column for each factor moment.

The original material computes these return-factor moments and uses them in a
cross-sectional regression. The code below keeps that logic and writes it
explicitly. The moment matrix is left on the original unscaled sum used in the
source material. A common rescaling would change the numerical coefficients and
preserve the fitted values from this regression.

```{r sdf-moment-regression}
sdf_source <- portfolio_factor_data |>
  select(date, symbol, Re, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML)

returns_wide <- sdf_source |>
  select(date, symbol, Re) |>
  pivot_wider(names_from = symbol, values_from = Re) |>
  arrange(date)

factors_wide <- sdf_source |>
  distinct(date, Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML) |>
  arrange(date)

common_dates <- intersect(returns_wide$date, factors_wide$date)

returns_matrix <- returns_wide |>
  filter(date %in% common_dates) |>
  select(-date) |>
  as.matrix()

factor_matrix <- factors_wide |>
  filter(date %in% common_dates) |>
  select(Mkt_RF, SMB3, HML) |>
  as.matrix()

moment_matrix <- t(returns_matrix) %*% factor_matrix

sdf_data <- as_tibble(moment_matrix, rownames = "symbol") |>
  rename(moment_market = Mkt_RF, moment_size = SMB3, moment_value = HML) |>
  inner_join(ff3_expected |> select(symbol, ERe_percent), by = "symbol")

sdf_fit <- lm(ERe_percent ~ moment_market + moment_size + moment_value, data = sdf_data)

sdf_data |>
  arrange(symbol) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 6))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Database for the SDF-moment cross-sectional regression.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

broom::tidy(sdf_fit) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 6))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Cross-sectional regression using return-factor moments.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

broom::glance(sdf_fit) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 6))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Goodness-of-fit summary for the SDF-moment cross-sectional regression.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")

```

The SDF-moment tables follow the same reading order as the beta method. The
first table contains the return-factor moments used as regressors. The
coefficient table estimates how those moments map into average excess returns.
The goodness-of-fit table summarizes the fitted cross-section for this
moment-based specification.

```{r}
#| label: fig-sdf-fit
#| fig-cap: "SDF-moment fitted returns."
sdf_aug <- broom::augment(sdf_fit, data = sdf_data)

sdf_aug |>
  left_join(portfolio_label_tbl, by = "symbol") |>
  ggplot(aes(.fitted, ERe_percent)) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = 2, color = "#D55E00", linewidth = 0.8) +
  geom_text(aes(label = symbol), size = 2.2) +
  coord_fixed(xlim = c(0.5, 1.65), ylim = c(0.5, 1.65)) +
  labs(
    x = "SDF-moment fitted return (%)",
    y = "Observed average excess return (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)
```

The SDF fitted-return figure should be compared with the beta-method fitted
figure. Both plots use the same vertical axis, observed average excess return.
The horizontal axis changes: the beta method uses fitted returns from estimated
betas, while the SDF method uses fitted returns from return-factor moments.

```{r sdf-fitted-table}
sdf_aug |>
  select(symbol, ERe_percent, fitted_return_percent = .fitted) |>
  arrange(symbol) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Observed and fitted average excess returns from the SDF-moment method.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The fitted-value table makes the SDF comparison explicit for each portfolio.
Together with the graph, it lets the reader see which portfolios are matched
closely and which remain difficult for the moment-based specification.

The beta method and the SDF-moment method use different regressors, but they
are both asking a cross-sectional asset pricing question: can a small set of
factor-related quantities explain the average excess returns of the test
assets?

The SDF-moment method uses products $R_i^e f_j$ as regressors. Each regressor
measures how a portfolio return co-moves with a factor in the sample.
The fitted values answer the same practical question as the beta method: given
the factor information, what average excess return does the model assign to
each portfolio?

```{r compare-cross-sectional-methods}
comparison_methods <- cross_section_aug |>
  transmute(symbol, beta_method = .fitted) |>
  inner_join(
    sdf_aug |> transmute(symbol, sdf_moment_method = .fitted),
    by = "symbol"
  ) |>
  inner_join(ff3_expected |> select(symbol, observed = ERe_percent), by = "symbol")

comparison_methods |>
  arrange(symbol) |>
  slice(c(1:5, (n() - 4):n())) |>
  mutate(across(where(is.numeric), ~ round(.x, 4))) |>
  kable(
    caption = "Fitted average excess returns from the two cross-sectional methods.",
    row.names = FALSE
  ) |>
  kable_styling(latex_options = "HOLD_position")
```

The final comparison table puts the two fitted values side by side. The reader
can inspect whether the two approaches agree for a portfolio or whether one
method assigns a noticeably different fitted average return. This closes the
empirical loop: time-series betas, cross-sectional beta pricing, and SDF
moments are now connected to the same 25 test assets.

## Implementation checks

The main implementation risk in factor models is losing the connection between
units, equations, and samples. These checks keep the workflow auditable:

| Check | Why it matters |
|---|---|
| Decimal returns in estimation | Regression coefficients assume returns and factors are in the same units. |
| Month-end dates | Asset returns and factors must align on the same calendar month. |
| Excess returns | Test-asset returns must subtract the risk-free rate before estimation. |
| Sample windows | FF3, momentum, and FF5 have different starting dates. |
| Intercepts | Alphas are pricing errors after controlling for the chosen factors. |
| Betas | Betas are exposures estimated from time-series regressions. |
| Cross-section | The second-pass regression tests whether estimated exposures explain average returns. |

The chapter deliberately keeps the code close to the equations. The data
download creates factor and portfolio returns. The time-series regressions
estimate $\alpha_i$ and $\boldsymbol{\beta}_i$. The fitted expected-return
equation converts those estimates into model-implied average excess returns.
The cross-sectional regressions then test whether factor exposures or
factor-moment quantities explain the 25 portfolio averages.

## Conclusion

This chapter extends the book from forecasting and volatility into asset
pricing econometrics. The object of interest is no longer a forecast of a
single future observation. The object is an estimated relation between average
excess returns and systematic risk exposures.

The empirical logic has three layers. First, factor returns define candidate
sources of systematic risk. Second, time-series regressions estimate each
asset's exposure to those factors. Third, cross-sectional regressions evaluate
whether those exposures explain average returns across test assets. This is the
core bridge from financial theory to econometric implementation.

The Fama-French chapter also illustrates a broader lesson for applied financial
econometrics: a model is useful only when the equation, data construction,
estimation code, and economic interpretation remain connected.
